translatedText: December 800
Received the title of Holy Roman Emperor from Pope Leo III at St. Peter's Basilica.
Winter of 801
Louis the Pious captured Barcelona.
Zodan, the Khagan of the Avars, visited Aachen, pledged allegiance as a vassal of Charlemagne, and converted to Christianity.
Croatian chieftains, including Višeslav, voluntarily submitted to him.
Byzantine Emperor Michael I Rangabe recognized Charlemagne as Emperor.
Declared his surviving son, Louis the Pious, as his legitimate successor at the court of Aachen and crowned him as co-emperor.
January 28, 814
Died of complications from pleurisy and depression at the Palace of Aachen.
Antipope Paschal III canonized Charlemagne at the request of Frederick I.
Expansion of the Frankish Kingdom
He greatly expanded the Frankish Kingdom by occupying most of western and central Europe, achieving the political unification of Western Europe.
Crowned Holy Roman Emperor
In December 800, he received the title of Holy Roman Emperor from Pope Leo III, politically and religiously uniting most of Western Europe for the first time since the Roman Empire.
Carolingian Renaissance
During his reign, he brought about the Carolingian Renaissance by greatly developing art, religion, and culture through the church.
Codification of Laws and Centralization
To rule his vast territory, he codified the laws of each tribe and dispatched officials from the center, enabling centralized rule.
Spread and Integration of Christianity
He sought ideological unification by converting conquered tribes to Catholicism and supported proselytization by dispatching missionaries.
Establishment of Cultural and Educational Institutions
He built a palace and royal chapel in Aachen, and established a royal library and a palace school, leading a revival of arts and sciences.
When was Charlemagne born?
There are theories that Charlemagne was born in 740, on April 2, 742, or in 747.
Why is Charlemagne called the 'Father of Europe'?
He is called the 'Father of Europe' because the Carolingian Renaissance laid the groundwork for the current identity of Europe.
When did Charlemagne become Holy Roman Emperor?
Charlemagne received the title of Holy Roman Emperor from Pope Leo III in December 800.
How tall was Charlemagne?
According to Einhard's records and the results of his tomb excavation in 1861, Charlemagne's height was about 190-191 cm.
What languages did Charlemagne speak?
In addition to his native language, he was fluent in Latin and could also speak Greek to some extent.
Where was Charlemagne born?
Charlemagne was born in Herstal, Wallonia, in the Frankish Kingdom near Liège, Belgium.
Reigned as the ninth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cuzco and the first Emperor of the Inca Empire.
Recognized as the successor to the throne for his merit in defending Cuzco during the Chanka invasion.
Laid the Foundation of the Inca Empire
He laid the foundation to develop the small Kingdom of Cuzco into the vast Inca Empire ruling the entire western South America.
Expansion of the Empire's Territory
During his reign, Qullasuyu and Chinchaysuyu, two of the four quarters of the Inca Empire, were brought under the empire's control.
Expansion and Renovation of the Capital Cuzco
He transformed Cuzco, which was merely a quiet provincial city, into a place befitting the capital of an empire through massive expansion.
Renovation of Coricancha
He renovated several Inca-era buildings, including the Temple of the Sun, Coricancha.
Implementation of Forced Relocation Policy
He implemented a colonial policy of forcibly relocating residents from the center of the empire to border areas to pioneer frontier regions.
What does Pachacuti's name mean?
In Quechua, it means "He who shakes time and space" or "Earth-shaker", and "Yupanqui" means "honor".
What was Pachacuti's position in the Inca Empire?
He was the ninth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cuzco and the first Emperor of the Inca Empire.
Who was Pachacuti's father?
His father was Viracocha Inca.
How did Pachacuti become the successor to the throne?
He was recognized as the successor to the throne around 1438 for his merit in defending Cuzco alone while his father and older brother fled during the Chanka invasion.
Who is believed to have built Machu Picchu?
Most archaeologists believe that Pachacuti built Machu Picchu.
October 25, 1542
Akbar was born
His father Humayun reconquered India
Crowned as the 3rd Emperor of the Mughal Empire at the age of 13
November 5, 1556
Defeated the Hindu army near Delhi
Renovated the old fortress of Agra and named it 'Akbarabad'
Started the construction of a new capital, Fatehpur Sikri, near Agra
Fatehpur Sikri began to be used as the capital
A large-scale rebellion broke out in opposition to a series of reforms
The capital was moved from Fatehpur Sikri to Lahore
Lahore was Akbar's capital
October 12, 1605
Akbar died
Development of the Mughal Empire
He developed the Mughal Empire founded by his grandfather Babur into a state worthy of a true empire.
Seizure of Power and Recruitment of Talent
Upon reaching adulthood, he took power himself and employed talented people regardless of their origins.
Territorial Expansion
He allied with and pacified Rajput queens, annexed most of northern India based on a new army, and expanded his domain southwards into the Deccan region towards the end of his reign.
Institutional Reform
He reformed the tax system, including abolishing the jizya (poll tax) imposed on non-Muslims under Islamic law.
Introduction of the Mansabdari System
He introduced the mansabdari system to rank the bureaucracy in order to secure stable military power.
Construction Projects
He completed his father Humayun's tomb, renovated the old fortress of Agra, renaming it 'Akbarabad', and started the construction of Fatehpur Sikri, which he used as his capital.
Religious Tolerance
He paid attention to various religions and embraced a religious attitude called 'Din-i Ilahi', comprehensively respecting different faiths.
Patronage of Culture and Scholarship
Although uneducated in his childhood, he loved art, patronized scholarship, and promoted the translation of Sanskrit works into Persian.
When was Akbar the Great born?
Akbar was born on October 25, 1542.
Which emperor of the Mughal Empire was Akbar the Great?
Akbar the Great was the 3rd Emperor of the Mughal Empire.
When did Akbar the Great ascend to the throne?
Akbar ascended to the throne in 1556 at the young age of 13.
What tax did Akbar the Great abolish?
Akbar abolished the jizya (poll tax) that was imposed on non-Muslims under Islamic law.
What military and bureaucratic system did Akbar the Great introduce?
Akbar introduced the mansabdari system to secure stable military power.
What major city did Akbar the Great build?
Akbar started the construction of a new capital, Fatehpur Sikri, near Agra.
What was Akbar the Great's religious view?
Akbar paid attention to various religions and adopted a religious attitude called 'Din-i Ilahi', which comprehensively respected various religions.
Around 1583
Nzinga Mbande was born
Seized power in Ndongo after the death of her brother Mbandi
Ruled the Kingdom of Ndongo
Portugal declared war on Ndongo
Nzinga's army became exhausted, leading to her exile
Rebuilt her army through the Imbangala alliance and conquered the Kingdom of Matamba
Ruled the Kingdom of Matamba
Allied with the Dutch West India Company, which captured Luanda from Portugal
Regained most of Ndongo
Portugal recaptured Luanda; the Dutch left Angola
Signed a peace treaty with Portugal
December 17, 1663
Nzinga Mbande died
Resolving Political Crises
She received military and political education in her youth and showed an aptitude for resolving political crises as an ambassador to the Portuguese Empire.
Conquest of the Kingdom of Matamba
After rebuilding her army through the Imbangala alliance, she conquered the Kingdom of Matamba between 1631 and 1635.
Recovery of Ndongo Territory
Through an alliance with the Dutch, she was able to recover most of Ndongo between 1641 and 1644.
Victory in Battles against Portugal
Allied with the Dutch, she defeated Portugal in several battles.
Which kingdoms did Nzinga Mbande rule?
Nzinga Mbande ruled the Ambundu Kingdoms of Ndongo (1624–1663) and Matamba (1631–1663), located in present-day northern Angola.
